data.title: Applied Digital: REIT Focus Is A Strategic Pivot For Growth
data.description:
Last December, I initiated my coverage on Applied Digital Corporation (NASDAQ:APLD) with a strong buy rating due to its potential to emerge as a leading provider of HPC AI data centers. Since then, the stock is down 58% as a result of delays to its multi-billion lease agreement with a US hyperscaler, leading to higher cash burn.

The stock’s drop was exacerbated by its recently announced Q3 FY 2025 earnings due to underwhelming revenue growth in its cloud services segment. At the same time, Applied Digital announced that it’s exploring opportunities to sell its cloud services segment, as part of its strategy to transition into a REIT in the future.

Despite this, I believe a sale of the cloud services segment could provide the company with ample cash to continue building out its Ellendale campus, which presents the biggest revenue opportunity thanks to demand from hyperscalers’ for data center capacity. In light of this, I’m reiterating my strong buy rating for Applied Digital, but lowering my price target to $47 by mid-2028, to account for the reduced revenue from the sale of the cloud services segment and the transition to a REIT, which still implies 1255% upside from current levels.

Q3 Overview

In Q3 FY 2025, Applied Digital reported 22% YoY revenue growth from $43.3 million to $52.9 million, driven by 220% growth in the cloud services segment from $5.5 million to $17.7 million, offset by a 7% decline in the data center hosting segment from $37.8 million to $35.2 million. While the growth in the cloud services segment may appear significant, it was a 36% sequential decline due to technical issues in moving GPU capacity from single-tenant reserve contracts to a multi-tenant on-demand model. Per management in the Q3 earnings call, these issues were resolved in the first or second week of March, which could see this segment generate revenues at a similar rate to Q2 during Q4.

In terms of costs, Applied Digital showed impressive cost management during Q3 as it reported a gross margin of 7.1% compared to -8.6% in the prior year.

Item

Q3 25

Q3 24

Revenue

$52,921,000

$43,348,000

CoR

$49,141,000

Gross Margin

7.1%

-8.6%

At the same time, SG&A costs declined 24.3% YoY from $30 million to $22.7 million, meaning that they represented 43% of revenues in Q3 2025 compared to 69.3% last year. Accordingly, the company’s operating loss improved by 65.8% YoY from $55.4 million to $18.9 million, at an operating margin of -35.8% compared to -127.9% in the prior year, a sign of improving efficiency.

Item

Q3 25

$52,921,000

Operating Loss

Operating Margin

-35.8%

-127.9%

In terms of cash flow, Applied Digital reported $5.9 million in operating cash flow, a substantial improvement from $52.3 million and $75.9 million in operating cash outflow in Q1 and Q2 2025, respectively. Meanwhile, CapEx reached $257.5 million in Q3 as the company ramps up its efforts to complete the build out of its Ellendale data center, compared to $171 million and $54.8 million in Q2 and Q1, respectively. As such, the company’s cash burn increased from prior quarters as free cash outflow came at $251.6 million in Q3 compared to $223.3 million and $130.7 million in Q2 and Q1, respectively.

Quarter

Q1 25

Q2 25

Q3 25

-$75,890,000

-$130,688,000

-$223,313,000

-$251,596,000

As for its balance sheet, Applied Digital exited Q3 with a cash balance of $68.7 million and had restricted cash of $185.5 million, including $154.1 million in funds for construction and $31.3 million in letters of credit. Meanwhile, the company has $689.1 million in total debt, of which $679 million are long-term debt, $386.1 million of which matures in FY 2027, bringing its net debt position to $434.9 million. Therefore, it is crucial for the company to finalize the lease agreement with the hyperscaler for its Ellendale data center in the near-term in order to deal with the debt mountain maturing in FY 2027.

REIT Transition

For the first time since announcing its strategic shift to building and leasing HPC data centers, Applied Digital’s management mentioned a future transition into a REIT, in the Q3 earnings call. According to this new strategy, the company has authorized the sale of its cloud services segment.

The company’s decision was driven by potential data center customers viewing this segment as a competitor, which has been a “point of friction” in negotiations, per management in the Q3 earnings call. In my opinion, this decision comes at an opportune time to capitalize on CoreWeave’s (CRWV) recent IPO. Currently, Coreweave is trading at a 4.96 sales multiple and Applied Digital’s management reiterated that they see the cloud services business to be a $110 to $120 million business.

At the midpoint of management’s expectations, and CoreWeave’s valuation, the cloud services segment could be sold for $570 million. These funds would provide the company with enough capital to continue building out its portfolio of HPC data centers, while minimizing its exposure to debt financing, reducing its interest expenses in the process.

By selling off this segment, Applied Digital vision of transitioning into a REIT would be possible since it will be mainly focused on operating its Bitcoin hosting and HPC data center segments, both of which fit into a REIT structure. In fact, management stated in the Q3 earnings call that the company may look to add more Bitcoin capacity along with HPC capacity, an endeavor that I expect to see demand from Bitcoin miners due to Bitcoin’s current prices.

In my opinion, Applied Digital’s new strategy is mainly driven by its partnership with Macquarie Asset Management. This partnership is a $5 billion perpetual preferred equity financing facility, with investment vehicles of funds managed by Macquarie, for the company’s HPC business. According to Applied Digital, Macquarie’s committed funds provide the majority of the funds needed to build more than 2 GW of HPC data center capacity.

At the same time, partnering with an institution as Macquarie validates Applied Digital’s HPC data center plans due to Macquarie’s reputation as a major financial institution. As is, management stated that Macquarie is aiding its negotiations with customers for the lease of the Ellendale data center, which may alleviate customer fears over Applied Digital’s ability to execute on its targets.

Revenue Projections

With Applied Digital exploring opportunities to sell off its cloud services business, I’m updating my revenue estimates for the company through FY 2028.

Cloud Services Segment

Starting with the elephant in the room, the cloud services segment generated $71.3 million in revenue YTD. As the technical issues that hindered the segment’s revenue generation during Q3 are now solved, I expect the segment to return to the same rate as Q2.

Clusters

6

Q2 Segment Revenue

$27,705,000

Revenue/Cluster

$4,617,500

This could see the segment generate around $99 million in FY 2025.

YTD Revenue

$71,313,000

Q4 Estimate

$27,705,000

FY 25 Rev Est

$99,018,000

Additionally, I expect the sale of this business to occur in Q2 FY 2026, at least, which could see this segment generate around $27.7 million in revenue in Q1 2026.

Cloud Services Segment

FY 25

$99,018,000

FY 26

$27,705,000

Data Center Hosting Segment

Per Applied Digital’s latest 10-Q filing, its 286 MW Bitcoin mining facilities are operating at full capacity and generate around $35 million in quarterly revenue. Accordingly, I’m estimating this segment to generate around $140 million in annual revenues, unless Applied Digital leases more capacity or renews its leases at a higher price.

Data Center Hosting Segment

FY 25

$140,000,000

FY 26

$140,000,000

FY 27

$140,000,000

FY 28

$140,000,000

HPC Data Center Segment

As for the HPC data center segment, Applied Digital is yet to realize revenues from this business as it continues to prepare the first building at the Ellendale data center to start operating soon. In the Q3 earnings call, management stated that the first building will be ready to generate revenues in Q4 CY 2025, or Q2 FY 2026. Meanwhile, the company expects the second building to be ready for operations at the end of Q2 CY 2026, or Q4 FY 2026, and the third building to be ready in Q1 CY 2027, or Q3 FY 2027.

With that in mind, my estimates assume the first building to generate revenues from Q2 FY 2026 and the second building to generate revenues in Q4 FY 2026. I’m also estimating the third building to start generating revenues in the second half of FY 2027.

My estimates are also based on the per MW pricing of the initial lease announcement of $2.2 million per year, as shared in my previous coverage on Applied Digital.

Accordingly, my revenue estimates for the HPC data center segment through FY 2028 are as follows.

HPC Segment

FY 25

$0

FY 26

$192,500,000

FY 27

$715,000,000

FY 28

$880,000,000

By adding my estimates for all segments, my revenue estimates for Applied Digital through FY 2028 are as follows.

Revenue Estimates

FY 25

$239,018,000

FY 26

$360,205,000

FY 27

$855,000,000

FY 28

$1,020,000,000

Valuation

Based on my revenue estimates, I believe Applied Digital as a bargain at its current share price of $3.44. At this valuation, the company has an EV of $1.2 billion, which translates to the following EV/sales multiples through FY 2028.

Fiscal Year

EV/Sales

2025

5.05

2026

3.35

2027

1.41

2028

1.18

In comparison, data center REITs Equinix (EQIX) and Digital Realty (DLR) are trading at the following EV/sales multiples.

Ticker

EV/Sales

EQIX

10.14

DLR

11.24

Average

10.69

By applying the average EV/sales multiple of both REITs to my revenue estimates for Applied Digital through FY 2028, my price target for the stock is as follows.

Technical Analysis

Looking at the TrendSpider chart above, Applied Digital appears to be in a bullish triangle pattern on the monthly chart. Currently, the stock is testing the lower trendline of the pattern, a level that has seen the stock previously run to test the resistance level at $10. As such, if Applied Digital maintains the lower trendline as support, I expect the stock to rebound near the $10 mark to test its resistance, which offers around 200% upside from the current stock price. Possible catalysts that could help Applied Digital retest the $10 resistance include the announcement of the lease agreement with a hyperscaler for the Ellendale data center and the sale of the cloud services business if the sale price is near my estimate, in my opinion.

Risks

Risks to my bullish thesis on Applied Digital include additional delays to announcing the lease agreement for the first building of the Ellendale data center. As mentioned earlier, the company needs to start generating free cash flow before FY 2027 when $$386.1 million of its debt matures. Therefore, additional delays to announcing the lease agreement could continue to pressure the stock price.

Another risk to consider is the recent tariffs imposed by the Trump administration. While the tariffs won’t impact the first building of the Ellendale data center, construction costs of the 2 additional buildings could increase substantially due to the tariffs. As is, management stated in the Q3 earnings call that its data center CapEx is between $30 to $50 million per month, and the added costs brought by the tariffs could see the company’s cash burn accelerate significantly.

Conclusion

In conclusion, I remain bullish on Applied Digital despite the delays in announcing the lease agreement for its Ellendale data center. With the first building near completion, I expect the lease agreement to be announced in the second half of 2025, especially with Macquarie involvement with the company, which validates its plans, in my opinion. Moreover, I expect Applied Digital to receive around $570 million from the sale of its cloud services segment, which will provide the company with much-needed liquidity to reduce its exposure to debt financing. After accounting for the loss of revenue from the cloud services segment, I’m reiterating my strong buy rating for Applied Digital at its current depressed valuation, while lowering my price target to $47 by mid-2028, implying 1255% upside from current levels.

data.title: CTA: Managed Futures ETF, Strong Diversifier Choice For Volatile Times
data.description:
The Simplify Managed Futures Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:CTA) is an actively managed, multi-asset class, long-short futures ETF. Positions and allocations are set through a proprietary model designed by Altis Partners, with subject matter experience. Returns are strongly dependent on the fund's investment strategy, less so on market returns: CTA is flat YTD, for instance. As such, an investment in the fund could serve to diversify, and de-risk an investor's portfolio, especially during times of market turmoil. CTA is a strong investment opportunity, and a buy.

CTA - Overview and Analysis

CTA is a multi-asset class, long-short, futures ETF. Let's have a closer look at what this entails. I wrote a similar explainer in my last article, and not much has changed about the fund's functioning.

Futures

CTA trades futures, derivative contracts with explicit characteristics and payment profiles. In simple terms, futures contracts are standardized legal agreements to buy or sell a particular commodity at a predetermined price and date.

Futures can be used to speculate on, and hopefully profit from, market movements. A long position in S&P 500 futures should be profitable if said equity index rises, and vice versa.

Futures are generally leveraged instruments, as they can be bought on margin / with only a small initial investment. Leverage amplifies gains and losses and could prove ruinous during severe market downturns or movements. CTA does not seem excessively leveraged, nor has the fund being significantly impacted by prior downturns.

Multi-Asset Class

CTA trades in futures from several asset classes, centered on commodities and rates. CTA does not trade equity or currency futures, for the following reasons:

Can't say I know enough about the long-term performance of these strategies to evaluate the above, but excluding equities has definitely led to reduced risk and volatility YTD. An aggressive equity futures trading strategy could have led to outsized gains following the tariffs announcement, but also to massive losses. CTA avoided most of these issues, which seems wise.

As CTA does not trade equity futures, performance could materially differ from that of the S&P 500 or other equity indexes. As an example, the fund is flat YTD, even as equity prices tumbled due to tariffs. CTA also avoided most of the gains as the tariffs were paused.

There have been other periods of time in which CTA's performance materially differed from that of the market. From a prior article, it seems the fund saw significant gains during April 2024, even as equity (and bond) prices drifted downwards.

Due to the above, CTA could be used as a source of portfolio diversification, and to reduce risk and volatility at the portfolio level. Pairing CTA with equities would have led to reduced losses YTD, for instance.

Long-Short

CTA employs both long and short futures positions. Direction depends on a suite of systematic, proprietary models developed by Altis Partners, a commodity trading advisor. Although I was unable to find any specific information about these models, other funds focus on trend following, and CTA implies the same.

As CTA is a multi-asset class, long-short fund, returns could be positive or negative during more or less any conceivable market scenario. CTA could see gains during a declining market, as was the case from January to March of this year.

CTA could also see losses when equity prices are declining, as was the case in the days following the tariffs announcement. Losses were much smaller, but the recovery has been slower too (albeit still to early to tell).

CTA could see gains during equity bull markets, as was the case during 2024.

CTA could see losses during equity bull markets too, as was the case during late 2023.

One could find similar behavior for the different asset classes. CTA's returns are not driven by market returns, but by its strategy, and the interplay of its positions and asset returns. In simple terms, if CTA goes long profitable assets and shorts unprofitable ones, it should generate strong, positive returns for shareholders. If it goes long underperforming assets and shorts the best-performing ones, it should see significant losses instead. Returns are strongly dependent on the effectiveness and success of its investment strategy, much more so than average.

Although the above might sound redundant or uninformative, most investments or ETFs act quite differently. The SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) should closely track the S&P 500 index, which should closely track the country's equity market. Same with the Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (QQQ) and most other equity ETFs. Differences in indexes, portfolios, and strategies might result in differences in returns, maybe even significant ones, but returns for these funds are strongly dependent on market returns and should hew to these. It would be implausible for equity markets to rise 20% and for SPY to tumble, for instance.

Outperformance and Diversification

Going back to CTA, one would expect the fund to outperform, or to have an easier time outperforming, during bear markets, due to more favorable comps. As an example, CTA's commodity trades could easily generate higher returns than the -8.0% that the S&P 500 has experienced YTD. These same trades would have a more difficult time generating the 24.9% in returns experienced by the S&P 500 during 2024. Equity market returns themselves do not necessarily impact CTA, but they do definitely have an impact when comparing their performance.

Due to the above, an investment in the fund should serve to reduce risk and volatility at the portfolio level. An investment in CTA would have reduced an equity investor's losses YTD, for instance. As the fund's returns are strongly dependent on the effectiveness of its investment strategy, this might not necessarily be true moving forward, but I do believe that it will be the case. It has been the case in the past too, which brings me to my next point.

Performance Analysis

CTA's performance track-record is quite good, with the fund marginally underperforming the S&P 500 since inception:

But with particularly strong performance during bear markets, including YTD:

And during 2022:

As an aside, and as implied above, I would always pair CTA with other investments / other asset classes. Some bonds for income, equities for returns, CTA to reduce volatility and drawdowns.

Conclusion

CTA is an actively managed, multi-asset class, long-short futures ETF. CTA's strategy has led to good returns in the past, especially during bear markets. I rate the fund a buy, and think it is a particularly useful tool to diversify, and de-risk, an investor's equity portfolio.

data.title: BigBear.ai Holdings: Great Story, Not Enough Cash (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

BigBear.ai Holdings, Inc. (NYSE:BBAI) is an overvalued business at 4x sales. Not only that, but I believe that it will need to raise additional funds in the coming few months.

Not only is there no clear path towards positive free cash flow, but the business is not a high-growth business, even though it does have a very compelling narrative.

Consequently, I rate this stock a sell.

Rapid Recap -- Such A Volatile Holding

Back in February, I wrote a piece titled, Do Facts Matter? Probably Not, where I said,

[...] not only is BBAI relatively light on cash in its balance sheet, but also its path to free cash flow positive is still a while away and unlikely to end 2025 free cash flow positive.

But is this overthinking matters? Personally, I'm not convinced that there's a positive risk-reward here, but at the same time, I'm removing my sell rating on this stock, and moving to the sidelines.

As an analyst, having a sell rating on a stock that was going up day after day was just too painful. It's a reminder that markets can remain irrational longer than you can remain solvent.

Today, I update my BBAI stock rating back to a sell rating.

BigBear.ai Holdings: Near-Term Prospects

BigBear.ai helps big organizations, especially in defense and national security, make smarter decisions using AI. They build software that looks at a massive amount of data, finds important patterns, and gives helpful insights that businesses can act on.

From helping the military with logistics or spotting issues in supply chains, BigBear.ai turns complex data into insights.

For my part, I've often stated that BigBear.ai is, in actual fact, more of a consulting business, rather than a SaaS platform, but let's not get too caught up in that detail.

In fact, I believe that there are so many big cloud companies offering largely similar AI tools to government agencies that this business doesn't have a meaningful moat.

Given this background on BigBear.ai, let's now delve into its fundamentals.

Growth Rates Are Not Those of A High-Growth Story

BigBear.ai wants to be viewed as a high-growth stock. But in reality, the business is anything but this.

Firstly, its guidance for 2025 points towards less than 20% y/y growth. That's rather meek. Indeed, there are many SaaS businesses around that are growing significantly faster than this.

Secondly, they are guiding for less than $200 million in revenues, and their growth opportunity is already moderating. Again, this business is many things, but it's not a secular growth story.

On top of that, keep in mind that Q1 of last year provides for a very easy comparable period. What happens to BigBear.ai's growth rates once the company is over the Q1 period, and its growth rates are up against the more challenging rest of the year?

It's likely that BigBear.ai's revenue growth rates could moderate down to around 12% y/y, once we get beyond Q1? This further supports my contention that this is not a growth business.

BBAI Stock Valuation -- 4x Forward Sales

As an Inflection investor, I believe that you should seek to back companies with strong balance sheets. And that companies with strong balance sheets are better placed to navigate financial difficulties.

As such, note that in early 2025, BigBear.ai raised around $65 million in cash from investors who used their warrants to buy shares. On top of that, $58 million worth of debt was converted into stock.

This means that BigBear.ai's net debt today stands at a more manageable approximate $30 million, down from a net debt position of $150 million at the end of Q4.

However, since BigBear.ai is probably going to burn through approximately $40 million of free cash flow in 2025, I suspect that sooner rather than later, BigBear.ai will have another round of capital raises. And in this market environment, raising capital will be a challenge.

Given all these financial considerations, investors are still willing to pay 4x forward sales for a business with no clear path towards free cash flow positive.

Arguably, the reason for that is that many investors are still price anchoring to a time, only a few months ago, when the stock was flying a lot higher, and reached $10 per share. Investors are looking back and thinking, if only the stock goes back to $5 per share, then there's a lot of money to be made here, in this small-cap stock.

But that's faulty reasoning. Why? Because a stock goes towards its future intrinsic value. What the stock was valued at before is irrelevant compared to where it's heading next.

Risk Factors the Upside

There's a lot of pessimism already priced into the market. Getting bearish at this junction doesn't add a lot of new information. We already know about the tariff war and the likely impact of shrinking business confidence. As such, a lot of negativity is already priced into share prices. Hence, getting negative at this point isn't that constructive.

Another aspect that could be viewed as bullish is that BigBear.ai held a special meeting for its shareholders on April 11, 2025, where they made convertible holders get paid out in shares, rather than in cash. This means that BigBear.ai will now be able to dilute shareholders to pay back debt holders, meaning that there's less near-term drag on its cash profile, so the business has bought itself a little more time.

The Bottom Line

Look, I get the excitement around AI and all the buzzwords BigBear.ai throws around, but when I actually dig into the numbers, it just doesn’t add up. This is a company that’s still burning through cash, guiding for underwhelming growth, and sitting at a 4x forward sales multiple.

That’s steep for a business that’s not showing a clear path to profitability.

Plus, there's a high likelihood they’ll need to raise more money soon—and in this market, that’s not going to be easy. In sum, I just can’t get behind it. I rate this stock a sell.

data.title: Karooooo: An Underestimated Company With A Rare Financial Profile
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Karooooo Ltd. (NASDAQ:KARO) is no different from Ituran Location (ITRN) that I previously covered. The two companies share a lot of similarities. Great Products. Excellent growth prospects. Operate in a market that is deemed lucrative, especially with changing trends and consumer lifestyles. They both serve the telematics industry. You can read more about ITRN and its similarities to KARO here.

I would say the market for telematic services is ripe. The Global telematics market is expected to reach $334 Bn by 2033 at a CAGR of 15.92%. According to research by Imarc, the growth is attributed to the need for enhanced fleet management and efficiency. Many fleet operators are constantly looking for solutions that will optimize fuel usage, improve route planning and minimize idle time.

During the release of 3Q FY2025 earnings results, the company has demonstrated a strong subscriber base, strong financials and an expansion of their products and services into other markets such as Southeast Asia, Africa and Europe. In as much as KARO is a small-cap company, it is operating at a rare financial profile. The company operates at a "Rule of 50-plus" based on 2025 GAAP street estimates as noted by CEO Calisto:

We believe we are among a select few SaaS companies operating at a "Rule of 50-plus" based on 2025 GAAP Street estimates. Notably, within a SaaS universe of approximately 200 companies, we believe we are one of only two small cap companies operating at this level.

With the global automotive telematics expected to grow at a CAGR of 10.1%, a solid financial profile and an edge in the telematics market, I believe KARO is well positioned to capitalize on its growth prospects.

Business Overview

Karooooo Ltd. operates through Cartrack and Karooooo Logistics. Cartrack is its operations management which operates as a SaaS platform with Karooooo Logistics is a delivery as a service (DaaS). Cartrack has a total of 2.2 Mn subscribers across 125,000 businesses. The segment generates $224 Mn y/y. On the other side, Karooooo Logistics facilitates a logistic and e-commerce scalable solution and contributed about $24 Mn y/y.

The company has a track record of growing its subscriber base at a sustainable and organic growth rate. In Q3, the total cartrack subscribers increased 17% y/y to a 2.2Mn subscriber base. This growth was attributed to expansion into underserved markets such as South Africa, Europe and Southeast Asia. KARO has also made significant investments to increase sales and coverage in South Africa and Southeast Asia. In late September, KARO opened an office in South Africa and management expects customer retention rate to remain at 95% as observed by CEO:

Finally, Cartrack delivered healthy subscriber additions in Q3 while maintaining strong unit economics with an LTV to CAC ratio greater than 9. Our commercial customer retention rate remains at 95%, and we continue to grow the business at scale with discipline.

The Catalyst

According to Forbes, AI is a game changer when it comes to fleet management in the following ways:

  • Unifying disparate data by integration for a holistic world fleet view and assisting in decision-making.
  • AI predicts equipment failures and enables proactive maintenance and reduces maintenance downtime.
  • AI constantly optimizes real-time route optimization while updating traffic, weather and driver data for efficiency.

Moving back to KARO, their end-to-end operations cloud offers the same characteristics. The cloud offers AI-powered cameras that monitor driver behaviors while reducing operational risks, leading to safer and more efficient fleet management. In addition, the cloud also offers field management tools, real-time mobile asset tracking, scalable e-commerce logistics, vehicle sharing and scheduling and driver risk analytics. All these features are integrated and are meant to achieve operational excellence, scalability and sustainable growth.

According to a case study conducted by KARO in South Africa, it was discovered that AI-powered decision-making can save lives. The case study showed that AI-powered decision-making could reduce fatigued driving by 32% as noted by the CEO:

For example, one of our customers in South Africa used our AI-powered cameras, combined with our fully digitalized coaching platform and actionable analytics, to achieve a 32% reduction in fatigue-related driving incidents, a 13% drop in mobile phone usage, and improved seat belt compliance-key contributors to preventing road fatalities.

In my opinion, the key growth catalyst is its adoption of AI-powered decision-making, its expansion to other markets such as Africa, Europe and Southeast Asia and its organic growth of subscribers while maintaining a strong customer retention rate. KARO has been growing its number of subscribers at 3.8% q/q. Using the same assumption, the company is expected to have grown their total number of subscribers to 4.8 Mn by 4Q FY30, as shown below.

The Opportunity

KARO remains committed to investing in product innovation, AI-driven solutions and increasing ROI for its customers. From a Profitability point of view, KARO scores an A- profitability grade. This was because of its growth in its EBITDA Margins of 47% and an increase in operating profit to $325 Mn from $274 Mn. Relative to its sector, the EBITDA Margins stood at 40.05%, which is 269% higher than the sector median of 10.83%

As KARO continues to focus on innovative products and growing its subscriber base, its growth prospects scored an A growth grade. Specifically, this grade was as a result of its end-to-end operations cloud, AI-powered decision-making services and products, expansion of its customer base in newer markets and increase in subscription sales to existing customers. With its new office in Southeast Asia, the company exposes its products and services to over 600 Mn people living in the Philippines, Thailand, Malaysia, Vietnam, Singapore and Hong Kong. Cumulatively, that's a $4 Tn thriving economy. In my opinion, KARO is well positioned to capture this significant and growth opportunity in a dynamic region that is also expected to lead in the telematics market as per ABI research.

Karo also has an A- revision grade and has had 2 upward revisions in the last 90 days as compared to zero downgrades.

Valuation

With regard to valuation, I would like to note that,

  • Karooooo is trading at a TTM PEG ratio of 0.81x, compared to 0.75x sector median.
  • In terms of forward EV/EBITDA, KARO trades at 11.22x, which is a discount of 6.4% compared to the sector median.
  • From a peer comparison point of view, KARO is trading below most of its peer TTM EV/EBITDA multiples. While KARO is trading at 12.38x, Exodus Movement (EXOD) is trading at 38.50x, Hurt 8 Corp. (HUT) is trading at 2.95x, Iren Limited (IREN) trades at 13.32x while Tuya (TUYA) and MeridianLink (MLNK) trade at -8.37x and 32.41x respectively.

While from a Valuation perspective, KARO scores a C- Valuation grade, there are a lot of other factors that suggest the company is greatly undervalued. In my opinion, the growth prospects of the company have still not been reflected in the share price. Additionally, the company is in the aggressive expansion stage, which could affect the valuation. Having said that, I wouldn't worry much about the current valuation grade.

Key Risks

The biggest risk for KARO is currency volatility, given that the company operates heavily in the South African Rand (ZAR). Additionally, with the current technological era, the emergence of self-driving cars is on the rise and could impact traditional fleet management solutions such as KARO. However, its management has shown that the company is keen to improve its product portfolio through constant innovation, and adoption of AI-powered products.

Final Thoughts

As mentioned earlier, KARO and ITRN share similar characteristics. Great companies, Solid and organic growth prospects. Profitable products and a transparent intention to create value for shareholders. KARO has also proven that it has a disciplined capital allocation framework and maintained that their top priority is organic growth and product innovation to continue its sustained profitability over the long run. Its profitability, revision, momentum and growth grades say it all.

data.title: Citizens Financial Group: More Signs Of Pain
data.description:
Citizens Financial Group, Inc. (NYSE:CFG) is a major regional bank we follow. As we have begun our regional bank coverage this earnings season, we have started to see some weakness in the regionals. Nothing disastrous, but some weakening to be sure. Last year, the bank tightened its lending standards, which helped stabilize asset quality, but we have seen more pressure on loans and deposits. We are remaining neutral on this bank rating it a sector perform. In this space on the weakness we are seeing we prefer to invest in higher-yielding bank stocks. Let us discuss Q1 results.

Citizens Financial Q1 Earnings Headline Performance

In the just-reported Q1 earnings release, Citizens saw mixed results on its loans and deposits. Revenues fell in Q1 from a year ago, with Citizens generating $1.94 billion in revenue. These revenues were a small decrease sequentially from $1.98 billion, and down 1% from a year ago. The provision for credit losses was $153 million, which was down from the sequential Q4's $162 million, and down $9 million from last year's Q1. Overall, provisions for losses have been stable largely over the last year. That is a relative positive sign.

The bank saw net income narrow from $401 million in Q4 to $373 million. However, this was up from $334 million last year. So, a bit mixed there, but EPS hit $0.77 and was a $0.02 beat against consensus expectations. While down from $0.85 in Q4, this was up from $0.65 a year ago.

Now, having followed this bank regularly, we will tell you that the one big issue we have had with the bank is that deposits and loans have declined for many quarters.

Citizems Financial Loans and Deposits

For the last two years, we have seen that total deposits have been on the decline. This quarter, a small positive was that deposits were up at $177.6 billion versus $174.3 billion in the sequential quarter. They were also up from $176.4 billion a year ago. This was positive. On the flip side, loans dipped on lower demand and more stringent lending criteria. That said, loans at period-end were down 1.0% from Q4.

This is a troubling trend. So total loans at the end of the quarter were $137.6 billion, down from $139.2 billion from the sequential Q4, but these are down from $143.2 billion a year ago. Loans have declined for over two years straight, and some of this is from tightening lending standards, but growth in loans and deposits are key. However, lower loans can be a positive if the asset quality is improving.

Citizens Financial Asset Quality and Return Metrics

We really have not seen asset quality improvement in the last two years, largely speaking. First, the net interest margin of 2.90% was up 4 basis points from Q4, which was positive. Further, net loan charge-offs rose from both Q4 and year-ago results. These were $200 million, $11 million higher than in Q4, and up from $181 million a year ago. Thus, net charge-offs were 0.58% as a percentage of all loans, up from 0.50% a year ago. As a percentage, non-accrual loans were 1.15% of all loans, and this was an improvement from 1.20% of total loans in Q4, but is up markedly from 1.02% a year ago.

So we are seeing lower loan volume, mostly flat deposits though up at period-end this quarter, and asset quality really has not improved. Margins ticked up a bit, but are nothing to write home about. Oh, and the efficiency ratio softened as well to 67.9%, from 66.3% in Q4. It is an improvement from last year's 69.3%. The return on average assets and equity both declined from Q4, but are up from a year ago. The underlying return on average tangible assets was 0.73% vs. 0.78% in the sequential quarter. The underlying return on average equity fell from 10.7% to 9.6%, and it still is down from 10.6% a year ago.

The Last Word

The performance metrics of Citizens Financial Group, Inc. continue to be mixed. We saw mixed asset quality and lower return metrics. We continue to see loans held fall, potentially hurting income potential. Deposits have long been trending lower, but we saw a bit of a tick up here in Q1. Overall, we rate this a sector perform, but see much better places to put money to work in the banking sector.

data.title: Spotify Is Executing Brilliantly, But The Stock Isn't A Buy Anymore (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
It's been a few quarters since I first analyzed Spotify Technology S.A. (NYSE:SPOT), whose rating I gave a “cautious buy”, given the mix of the company's potential and initiatives that were being carried out with some structural risks in the thesis. Since then, SPOT stock has advanced 85.1%.

When I resumed coverage, I noticed that the improvements made by the company were much better than I had estimated, which made it possible to increase conviction in the company and its execution.

On the other hand, 85% gains also reduce the margin of safety, and now require greater optimism in order to find a good value for Spotify's valuation.

Spotify Execution Was Brilliant

The latest quarters reported by Spotify have forced me to revise my assumptions for the thesis. Previously, I conservatively expected Spotify to reach a gross margin of over 30% and 750 million MAUs in a few years' time, with a gradual increase in the revenue mix.

Now, in the outlook for Q1 2025, the company expects 678 million MAUs and a gross margin of 31.5%, as well as an operating margin of 13%. This has been achieved through a series of well-executed management initiatives. For example, in 2024 there was the largest ever Wrapped, which shows the high engagement that the platform has, as well as the maturing of podcasts and other programs for the platform's creators.

On the profitability side, gross margin, and especially premium gross margin increased significantly, supported by audiobooks, music, and other costs of revenue. This was one of the main points of uncertainty in the thesis since the dependence of major artists on record labels such as Universal Music Group N.V. (OTCPK:UMGNF) and Warner Music Group Corp. (WMG) added additional pressure from royalties, but the company has proved that it can improve this margin through not only scale but also diversification.

Even with KPIs and revenue advancing consistently, the company has still shown that it can reduce expenses. I'm not just talking about reducing expenses in relation to revenue, which would be enough to improve margin, but reducing operating expenses in total, which is very difficult and reflects how the company is managing to capture efficiency through decreases in personnel costs and marketing.

This brilliant execution is not over yet, as mentioned by the CEO, 2025 will be the “year of accelerated execution”, with the company still having many initiatives to mature further such as higher-priced premium tier and video features.

Spotify’s Risks Still Exist

Would it be possible to say that all this quality in recent earnings and solid prospects mitigate the risk? Certainly. The problem is that this is far from completely mitigating the risks or making the long run very clear.

Even with the diversification in revenue, new features, and the like, Spotify is still a music company and therefore depends on agreements with record labels. What I mean by this is that there is a limit to the increase in gross margin, even if the prospects are strong as the company diversifies and improves the revenue mix, possibly adding more tiers, I don't think we'll see a gross margin much higher than it is now. On the other hand, any percentage point increase is relevant and makes a difference to the thesis.

A second factor to keep an eye on, and one that removes some visibility of growth in the long run, is the competition added to the addressable market. Spotify is already approaching 700 million users, and although the addressable market is much larger than that (virtually every person in the world who owns a smartphone or computer), it's already a very high number.

I'm not saying that the company can't reach 1 billion users in the next few years, in fact, I believe that this is possible with regions like LatAm and the “rest of the world” increasing penetration, which is possible through the freemium model. However, each year we project it will become more difficult to add millions of users in the quarter, especially if we think that services like YouTube Music, included in the YouTube subscription, Amazon Music, or Apple Music, are also competing in this industry, and some like YouTube are becoming compelling.

The bottom line here is that although it is possible to envision a beautiful future for Spotify in the coming years, there is a limit to this, and caution is required.

Revisiting the Math Behind Spotify

To draw up new assumptions for Spotify, let's use the annualized Q4 as a basis. For users, considering an increase of 6% per year, it is possible to find 850 million subscribers in 2028. As already mentioned, this number is quite reasonable, since the addressable market is quite large. Meanwhile, I see this assumption as slightly optimistic, given that the company will need to add around 44 million users a year in order to achieve this number.

For revenue per user, the prospects are clearer, since the conversion of free users to premium users, and up-selling by creating new tiers and new features, should boost this factor. So we're going to use €40 per user per year. This may seem like a very high jump, but consider that if 40% of the 850 million users are premium and pay an average of $100 per year, it's already possible to reach a revenue of €34 billion, so I think this assumption is very reasonable.

Finally, let's consider an increase of almost 6 percentage points for the operating margin, reaching 17%, which should be possible through the best revenue mix and continuous efficiency capture. This implies a price-to-operating income of 17x the current market cap.

This multiple is not bad, especially considering that there are tons of optionalities that Spotify can do to improve margin and revenue per user. But I don't think the assumptions used are bad enough to leave much room for positive surprises, i.e., the margin of safety here is not as great as it might seem.

The Bottom Line

Since I started covering Spotify, the thesis has gotten better, with the market pricing the SPOT stock better. That's why, after 80% gains, I understand that the margin of safety is not what it was a few quarters ago, and it's necessary to be more optimistic and count on optionalities and positive surprises to justify better returns for shareholders, even with some relevant risks in the very long term.

I, therefore, downgrade SPOT stock from a “cautious buy” to a “cautious hold” until the margin of safety returns to normal.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SK Telecom Is The Flight To Telecom Safety We Were Looking For
data.description:
Last week I wrote up an article about KT, the Korean Telecom company that I didn’t care for looking at the flight to safety. Their margin was falling, and they kept missing earnings estimates. So, then I got to thinking, if that’s not a good flight to safety telecom, what is?

Today we’ll be looking at one of KT’s competitors, SK Telecom Co., Ltd. (NYSE:SKM). It keeps showing up in my screeners as relatively cheap on a sector basis for telecom companies. Will it stack up as well as it looks initially? We will see.

SK Telecom is the largest mobile provider in South Korea, ahead of their main competitors KT and LG U+. SK Telecom holds about 40% of the market share, while each of the other two is in the range of 30% each (per the most recent 20-F). Though they continue to fare quite well, beating earnings estimates once again in 2024 (the third straight year they’ve done so), SK Telecom is also looking at revamping parts of their business to focus heavily on AI. We’ll be talking about their AI push and what it’s shaping up to look like, along with the numbers from the past fiscal year.

Earnings Surge in 2024 on Improved Operations

2023

2024

Operational Revenue

$12.4 billion

$12.6 billion

Operating Income

$1.23 billion

$1.28 billion

Net Income

$803 million

$1.01 billion

GAAP EPADS

$2.10

$2.64

(Source: February 6-K from SEC).

SK Telecom has been envisioning AI as the main driver of future revenue growth, but as you can see here, it hasn’t really started happening just yet. The company isn’t a grower, but they did have a massively improved net income number in 2024 because of a higher continuing operating income, which was up about 4% year over year.

The growth trend is expected to continue to be absent in 2025, and in the most recent conference call, SK Telecom said they expect revenue to be roughly flat, with some more improvements in operating income from further improvements in their operations.

2024 numbers translate to a GAAP P/E ratio of 8.41 at current prices, which is strongly better than the sector median of 18.44. This is a good start for the case that SK Telecom may be a strong value option.

Estimates for 2025 are revenue of $12.48 billion and non-GAAP EPADS of $2.84. Once again that’s quite a bit better than the sector, and this estimate might be on the low side, given that SK Telecom projected flat revenue, and further improved operating income. Given that, it’s hard to imagine the non-GAAP EPADS would fall from 2024’s number of $4.24 all the way to $2.84 on flat revenue. SK Telecom has beat estimates three straight years, and might well be shaping up to do it again.

The continued improvement of operating margins is obviously welcome, but what about revenue growth? For that, we’re going to have to take a close look at SK Telecom’s AI efforts because all indications are that’s where the growth will be coming from.

SK Telecom’s “AI Pyramid Strategy”

SK Telecom has designs on not just being a player in the global AI revolution, but one of the big ones. That may be ambitious, but they unveiled a plan called the Pyramid Strategy back in 2023 which gives us an idea of where they see this effort heading.

The company is now operating in two distinct sectors, mobile and AI, obviously with the former being a lot more lucrative than the latter right now. The hope is that will change, and if they can grow AI as they believe, it could well be something to watch. That doesn’t come free, though.

SK Telecom has said that they intend to triple the rate of investment in AI from the 2023 beginning of the strategy to 2028, up to KRW 25 trillion per year, which amounts to $17.6 billion US. These sectors aren’t totally independent either in the near term, with expectations that the AI sector will be able to strengthen their mobile and broadband offerings. It’s an AI world now, and it’s unsurprising SK Telecom is diving right in. They’re doing so in a big way too.

With regard to the B2C part of the business, they’re working on large-language model AI personal assistants. This includes a global goal of the GPAA or Global Personal AI Agent, but more significantly right now is “A.”.

A., or A dot as it is sometimes known, is an AI-powered personnel assistant capable of conversational interaction. The plan is to launch a paid version of A. later this year, and there’s a lot of excitement surrounding it. What’s out right now is considered quite impressive, and there’s big hope that it’s going to be the first big AI success story for the company. According to the conference call, they’ve already seen a 160% increase in A. users year-over-year, reflecting the momentum that’s starting to develop.

On the B2B side of things, they have a broad strategy called AIX, which aims to integrate AI advancements into business operations and expand cloud service. They have been partnering with multiple AI companies to that end.

The biggest effort on B2B so far is at the AI data center in Gasan, which is near Seoul. Under a partnership with Lambda Technologies, who SK Telecom has also invested in, they have substantially upgraded the Gasan data center, and have launched a GPU-as-a-service offering, which they hope will be embraced by various companies investing in AI development.

AI is an exciting topic, and SK Telecom is working with a lot of different companies to rise to the fore in it. It may be quite some time before this sector is profitable for them, but it shows promise, and they are investing big. Currently, the GPU-as-a-service offering and the A. paid edition are likely to be the focus in 2025, but there’s a constant effort to find different avenues of relevant growth here.

Dividends and Stock Buybacks, a High Return Stock

Dividends are a big part of the allure of telecom companies, and that’s a big part of why they’re traditionally thought of as a flight-to-safety choice in economic uncertainty. Even KT, which as mentioned before I didn’t really care for, has nearly a 4% yield, roughly in line with the sector median. SK Telecom looks even better in this regard.

They make their dividend payments in KRW, so it varies somewhat depending on the conversion rate. In general, they pay around 33¢ per quarter per ADS. This translates to a yield of 5.9%, quite a bit above the sector median. Then there’s also the possibility of stock repurchases in the future.

In their conference call, SK Telecom said the priority is to maintain dividend stability, but that they may also enter into share repurchase programs if stock trends warrant it. Currently, their price/book ratio is 1.02, better than the sector’s 1.82, so the buyback wouldn’t be a terrible idea. Obviously, cash needs to be preserved to invest in AI growth, but if they’re able, a buyback would be a great way to enhance returns for investors. Even if they don’t, the well above average dividend yield is already a reason to find this appealing.

South Korea’s Economy and the Risks of Going All-in on AI

Risk-wise, SK Telecom is not looking that dissimilar from KT, though without the problem of falling margins or repeated earnings misses. The big concern, as with everyone else, is what is to come of the global economy in the near term with a growing tariff war.

Now much like KT, SK Telecom isn’t a big export company or anything, but exports are quite important to the South Korean economy, and a recession would be a problem for virtually everyone. Though SK Telecom’s stock price might benefit from the perceived flight to safety factor, their earnings might be challenged if the whole economy is suffering.

Then there’s the AI factor. As mentioned before, SK Telecom is investing heavily in AI, and while there’s a lot of hope they’re making some progress in that end, we still don’t know what the future of AI will look like, nor if heavily investing in it right now will pay off in the long run. I don’t blame them for making such an effort to try to restart company revenue growth, but we should understand it comes with risks.

The Right Flight to Safety Stock

In my view, SK Telecom is a much, much better than KT as a flight-to-safety stock, and its numbers suggest there is a strong case to be made for it to be an income stock or a value stock. Though there are a lot of questions remaining about how the investment in AI will pan out, even that is looking encouraging so far, though it may well be years before we see real returns on that investment. I’m rating this a strong buy.

If you’re looking for a telecom stock or exposure in South Korea, you could do a lot worse than SK Telecom. Adding to the appeal, it isn’t trading near a 52-week high like a lot of stocks that have already experienced a flight-to-safety surge. SK Telecom is trading around the middle of its 52-week range.

Investors should keep a close eye on the GPU-as-a-service business in future quarters because that seems like it’s the most likely part of their AI sector to take off early. Watching progress on AI is also important because if it continues to grow the way it did in 2024, they could really have something there too.

data.title: MPLX Is A Strong Income Investment With Large Distributions And Growth
data.description:
The recent volatility has created an opportunity for investors with cash on the sidelines. Many companies have experienced significant retracements and income investments, such as MPLX LP (NYSE:MPLX), which are trading at enticing yields. MPLX has built an energy infrastructure company that connects several basins to the major markets in addition to terminals, refineries, and export facilities. As the demand for energy grows, MPLX is likely to experience increased levels of Adjusted EBITDA and Distributable Cash Flow (DCF), which will allow them to allocate more capital toward growth projects and increase the distributions going forward. MPLX is yielding close to 8% as its distribution is paying unitholders $3.83. The United States remains the largest producer of oil globally, and the upstream production of oil and gas will remain a critical component of the global economy as more individuals increase their standard of living and the age of artificial intelligence and robotics advances. I am looking at income investments with protected moats, and energy infrastructure is at the top of the list. Anyone can raise capital and try to compete in the software space, but you can't raise capital and decide to build pipelines. MPLX is an energy tollbooth, and I believe it's trading at a discount. Investors are able to lock in strong yields at these levels, with future distribution growth on the horizon.

Following up on my previous article about MPLX

Since I turned bullish on MPLX last July, shares have increased by 19.05%. Since the last article that I wrote in the middle of January (can be read here) MPLX has mitigated downside risk better than the market as its units have declined by -2.18% while the S&P 500 has declined by -10.26%. The total return for MPLX has been -0.34% since then, after the distribution is factored in. In that article, I discussed why I was bullish after Q3 earnings, and how I believed the macroeconomic landscape was setting up well for MPLX. Now that the 2024 fiscal year is over, I am following up with a new article, as I believe MPLX is in a strong position to benefit from increased revenue and profitability as the demand for energy continues to grow. Now that we have the data from the mega caps around CapEx spending and chip production, I believe that the bull case for MPLX is stronger than ever. With the expansion expected to occur, more traditional energy sources will be needed, and MPLX will be a clear beneficiary as the years progress.

Risks to investing in MPLX

Just because I am very bullish on MPLX and other energy infrastructure companies, that doesn't mean that these investment ideas will work out how I envision they will. Upstream production in the United States could stall and then decline, which would directly impact MPLX as less fuel would be moving through their system. If the demand for energy declines, then MPLX will have trouble contracting its pipeline capacity in the future. If the advancements in technology plateau, then there could be a slowdown in CapEx spending as the mega caps will no longer be in competition for the latest GPUs as they focus more on utilizing their current infrastructure. If this occurs, the projected forecasts for the demand of energy would likely decline, and MPLX would be directly impacted. MPLX also faces operational risks, and if they experience pipeline leaks, it could lead to unprecedented environmental damage, which could become costly from both a capital and reputation standpoint. MPLX could also underperform the market, as it has over the past 5-10 years. If the story around the demand for energy changes, MPLX may not be able to grow its distribution at the same pace and could be forced to cut the distribution at some point. Investors should do their own research, as investing in MPLX is not a sure bet.

Tax information

MPLX is a limited partnership that issues a Schedule K-1 tax package. These are normally mailed to their unitholders in mid-March. Anyone who is a current unitholder or interested in investing in MPLX should read through the MPLX Tax Reporting Package (can be found here) and discuss with a tax professional how this may impact your taxes.

MPLX is an income investor's dream, and the current valuation presents a strong opportunity

Income investors typically look for high-quality yields rather than outsized returns. It's a different mindset as they are focused on recurring income that is growing rather than trying to match or beat the market. There are two schools of thought regarding income investing: some investors look for large yields, while others look for annualized growth. MPLX offers something of both as the current yield is almost 8%, and MPLX has increased its distribution for the past 11 years. MPLX offers a high-quality distribution, as it's protected by fee-based contracts from commodities that are essential to the economy. MPLX has been able to grow its distribution through recessions and the pandemic, while others had to cut or stop their annualized increases. I believe that MPLX is likely to continue increasing the distribution as they continue to grow their cash from operations and bring more projects online to facilitate the increased demand for their services.

Since going public at the end of 2012, MPLX has generated a tremendous amount of income for its investors. At the end of 2012, MPLX units traded for $32.25 and generated $30.08 in distributed income. The quarterly distribution has grown by 440.70% from $0.18 to $0.96. Over the past 5-years, MPLX has increased its distribution at an average of 6.08%, and its latest increase took the quarterly distribution from $0.85 to $0.96. This is a high-quality distribution that has allowed investors to benefit from both the amount of income it produces and the annualized growth.

The latest CPI report came in at 2.4%, as inflation has fallen from 3.5% over the past year. Unemployment has gone the opposite way, as it has increased from 3.9% to 4.2% over this period. CME Group is expecting the Fed to cut at least 3 times in 2025, with more than a 40% chance that the Fed cuts at least 4 times. In an environment where the Fed is cutting and the risk-free rate of return declines, the distribution yield from MPLX will look more enticing to income investors because the base yield will double the risk-free rate of return with historical annualized increases. I think there is a strong opportunity for income investors with units of MPLX trading for around $50, especially since they have been bringing projects online to drive higher amounts of cash flow from their operations.

MPLX posted a strong 2024 fiscal year, and 2025 has a lot of potential to see increases across the board

MPLX made a lot of progress in the 2024 fiscal year. MPLX finished Q4, generating $1.1 billion in net income and $1.48 billion in DCF. This allowed MPLX to have a distribution coverage ratio of 1.5x during Q4 based on the quarterly distribution of $0.96. In the 2024 fiscal year, MPLX was a cash flow machine, which generated $5.9 billion in cash from operations and $5.7 billion in distributable cash flow. MPLX's revenue increased 4.21% ($450 million) to $11.13 billion and produced $6.33 billion in gross profit, which was an increase of 5.05% ($304 million). MPLX is operating a high-margin business with a 56.84% gross profit margin and large margins on the other profitability metrics. MPLX's EBITDA increased 4.64% YoY to $5.77 billion at a margin of 56.84%. MPLX generated $4.89 billion in FCF during 2024, which was a YoY increase of 9.64% ($430 million), while its net income grew 9.9% ($389 million) to $4.32 billion. MPLX was able to produce a 43.93% FCF margin and a 38.78% profit margin. This is impactful based on the operations that came online and the pipeline of new projects funded by their retained DCF.

I'm excited about MPLX's growth strategy, as it's expected to allocate $1.7 billion toward new projects that are expected to come online in 2025. The Gulf Coast Fractionation Complex will be in partnership with ONEOK (OKE). This project will strengthen the NGL value chain for MPLX by increasing fractionation capacity and its export capabilities. MPLX has been diversifying its revenue mix into natural gas and natural gas liquids (NGL) services, and this endeavor will add to its diversification. The BANGL pipeline expansion is expected to boost its capacity to 250,000 bpd of NGLs from the Permian Basin. The expansion is expected to be completed and brought online in Q1, 2025. The last project I am excited about this year is the Secretariat Processing Plant, which will increase MPLX's gathering and processing segment. This will be MPLX's 7th plant in the Permian. These projects show MPLX's commitment to its growth strategy and diversifying its revenue mix away from crude. MPLX has grown its DCF by 6.9% CAGR since the close of 2021, adding $1.1 billion in DCF. This has allowed them to grow the distribution at a 10.7% CAGR since the 2021 fiscal year, as the distribution has grown by 10% or more in each of the last 3 fiscal years.

When I look at the EIA Short-Term Energy Outlook, the total production and consumption of liquid fuels is expected to increase through 2026. The EIA is also expecting that the amount of energy consumption in 2025 and 2026 will increase compared to prior years. This is mainly due to the number of data centers coming online, and MPLX is in a great position. As the demand for energy increases, the projects MPLX is bringing online should be instantly accreditive to earnings, and we could have several more years of continued top and bottom-line growth in the financials. MPLX provides critical infrastructure, and based on the EIA projections, there should be more than enough demand for the operations that come into production from MPLX in 2025.

Conclusion

I think there will be a lot of opportunities for MPLX in the coming years. As the demand for energy continues to grow, it should provide MPLX with the ability to drive larger amounts of cash flow from its operations. MPLX has been an income investor's dream, and investors now have another opportunity to add shares at around the $50 level. MPLX is yielding around 8% with a distribution growth rate that exceeds a 6% CAGR over the past 5-years and a 10% CAGR over the last 3-years. MPLX could be in a position to continue growing the distribution at these rates, and in an environment where the Fed will be cutting the large yield, it will look very enticing compared to the deteriorating risk-free rate of return. MPLX is operating a high-margin energy infrastructure business, and more than 30% of every dollar of additional revenue generated goes to the bottom line. I expect MPLX units to rally throughout the year, and we will likely see another large increase in the distribution in November.

data.title: Target Is A Value Target
data.description:
The investment thesis for Target Corporation (NYSE:TGT) is very strong for a value investor. Contra positions can be considered at a time, when the value outshines the risks to the thesis (and there are a few). Let us see why.

Several Key Indicators are Looking Great

Gross profit margins have been around the just sub 30% mark, which is great. The RoE is standout – at close to 27%. Free Cash Flow is also looking healthy – one of the healthiest among retailers for its revenue size. At $3.7b, the levered free cash flow for 2024, is significantly higher than the $2.3b reported in 2023. This is a continued improvement after the cash flow impact in 2022, where TGT was hit on discretionary spending – stocked up inventory anticipating strong demand continuation post-COVID. This was also a period of heavy capex investment by TGT on revamping infrastructure and going bigger on ecommerce fulfillment.

Some may argue that these are lagging indicators – what matters is how TGT will react to the likely upcoming drop in discretionary spending and rising prices due to tariffs. Firstly, TGT’s similar experience not so far back in 2022-23 should hold them in good stead this time round. Even in the case of a deeper and wider impact this time, look at two things to derive comfort. The prices have already corrected anticipating this downturn (sure, they could correct even further, but unlike in 2022, TGT's share price seems more prepared). Secondly, valuation. At a forward PE ratio of 10.21, TGT is trading at a deep discount in comparison to its own 5-year historical average of 18.58 (and a sector median of ~18). These factors were not the same in 2022-23. TGT has braced for impact. It is very possible there will be an impact, but TGT may be better positioned to ride through it.

TGT has a high dividend yield of 4.83%, great for cushioning the ride as you hold TGT for the valuation multiple expansion from an economic rebound (or simply valuation adjustment on threat reassessment). The dividend was not impacted in the last stress period of 2022-23 (stress for TGT) and the dividend outlook remains good for the upcoming years, as the strong free cash flow history suggests decent financial stability to fund dividends.

Risks to the Thesis

One, an overall economic downturn that impacts retail across the board. Two, a hit on discretionary spending impacts TGT more than essential retailers and competitors. Three, Amazon and Walmart’s competitive pressures along with discount retailers such as Costco. Four, political and social boycotts have likely been impacting TGT sales in the recent past. And Five, tariff impacts on supply chain disruptions and rising prices to be passed on to customers (or eating into the margins).

Major headwinds those! On one and two, recent learnings, strategy, and the bounce back from the lows of 2022-23 should prove valuable for TGT. WMT and AMZN did not go through the same levels of stress as TGT, and I expect that to reflect in how TGT reacts. Three is real – particularly shifts to discount retailers or non-discretionary offerings are a TGT-specific threat compared to competition. Four and Five are not specific to TGT. Five, in particular, is a two-edged sword. While all retailers are reviewing contracts and realigning strategic supply chains, there is a possibility that the markets may have assumed and priced in the worst. Any positive developments could set a reversal in action (although even the temporary damage is bad enough).

The major question is how much of the risks, particularly the ones specific to TGT’s discretionary offering are already factored in. We cannot time the markets and don’t know the exact extent of the tariff crisis or its play-out impact time. I am not underestimating it at all – developments and repercussions are huge at face value. All I am saying is that, value investing is often a contra bet – waiting for the rebound may mean missing the bus. I would not argue the same to be true for other stocks that have not shown the same levels of valuation discounts (and yes, I know that discount is for a reason – a strong one). What tilts me to value buy TGT, at least from a long-term lens, is the correction already in place. TGT is trading at around 5-year low levels – it is hard to ignore a Buy call at this juncture on valuation. Fundamentals are intact, dividends are in play.

Long Term Target

A worrying trend for TGT has been stagnation in the growth of revenues and its absence from global markets – unlike its peers Walmart and Amazon. So what is TGT doing to address long-term prospects?

Target Plus Marketplace – the third-party digital, is working on a strategy to include new brands and attract a stronger customer base with aims to grow the business five-fold over the next few years. Investments are also in place to boost digital revenue streams from Roundel. Store expansion and remodeling are cushioned by the huge free cash flow (and debt – TGT has a high debt-to-equity ratio, even for a physical retailer, but not concerning levels). Some investments were already made in the stress period of 2022-23 when Same Day Deliveries were strengthened (Shipt acquisition in 2017 and stepping up the drive in 2022-23). AI and technology to improve the customer experience is also in. All the usual suspects for a physical retailer! How TGT executes those plans – been good so far – will be key.

Why I Like TGT

Other than valuations, dividends, and strong financials, I see a quiet technology revolution in action. TGT is already using AI for not only the customer experience but also for internal training, supply chain optimizations, and pricing. Their tech stack is completely owned in-house – no reliance on third parties. I also see TGT as a brand incubator – not just retail. Brand launches specifically targeting when there's a wider downturn in discretionary is not a difficult ask for TGT.

Anything below $95 is a buy for TGT, for a target of $150. Dividends are expected to be in the vicinity of 4.5%. Don’t miss this contra opportunity!

data.title: TG Therapeutics: Progress In MS Tempered By High Expectations
data.description:
Overview

Multiple sclerosis (MS) drug developer, TG Therapeutics (NASDAQ:TGTX) is up another 25% since my rating downgrade in January. While the firm’s intravenous anti-CD20 therapy, Briumvi (ublituximab), was carving a niche in the competitive MS market, its plans for a subcutaneous version seemed late to the punch. So, my Hold rating was really a balancing act between short-term gains and long-term challenges.

The company has since reported its full year 2024 earnings, so I figure this is a good time to reevaluate.

Recent Developments

In Q4 and FY24, Briumvi generated $103.6 million and $310 million in revenue, respectively. So, Briumvi is still accelerating following its January 2023 launch. As for 2025, TGTX guided Briumvi revenues to be “approximately $540 million.” Meanwhile, Briumvi launched in the EU. Its partner, Neuraxpharm, spearheads marketing efforts.

TGTX continues to strengthen Briumvi’s clinical data and administration convenience. On the former, open-label five-year data revealed that 92% of Briumvi patients were “free from disability progression.” Regarding administration convenience, 30-minute Briumvi infusions were well tolerated in a Phase 3b trial. Currently, Briumvi is administered over an hour, which compares favorably over competitors like Roche’s (OTCQX:RHHBY) Ocrevus (2-4 hours).

Recently, TGTX began a Phase 1 trial evaluating subcutaneous ublituximab in patients with relapsing forms of MS. So, TGTX is aiming to compete directly with Novartis’ (NVS) blockbuster Kesimpta. This is smart because subcutaneous solutions are growing in popularity in the MS market due to convenience. Despite being approved nearly five years ago, Kesimpta is still seeing massive growth. It generated $3.224 billion in 2024 (up 49% year-over-year). Currently, Kesimpta can be administered at home via an auto-injector pen. Patients are instructed to take it weekly for the first three weeks, skip a week, and then administer it monthly. In September 2024, Roche, too, entered the subcutaneous market with the approval of Ocrevus Zunovo. However, Ocrevus Zunovo requires an office visit twice a year for its 10-minute administration.

TGTX believes subcutaneous ublituximab's efficacy can hold up if administered every other month. This would offer a convenience advantage over Kesimpta. On the flip side, TGTX may come across a number of barriers. For starters, ublituximab’s efficacy will have to match or exceed Kesimpta’s despite less frequent administration. If approved, ublituximab then needs to overcome Kesimplta’s significant first-mover advantage (i.e. established prescriber familiarity and patient experience). Notably, Novartis is, too, building the case for Kesimpta with six year data demonstrating its efficacy and safety in first-line and switch patients.

TGTX is also exploring other autoimmune applications for subcutaneous ublituximab, like myasthenia gravis (MG). This is in Phase 1 development. Anti-CD20 utilization is not new for MG. There is evidence that rituximab, another anti-CD20, is effective in select MG patients (MuSK-positive), but its off-label use is limited to refractory cases. Notably, it has been estimated that only 5-8% of MG patients are MuSK-positive.

TGTX has been working to expand its product offerings beyond ublituximab. With its partner, Precision BioSciences, it is evaluating azer-cel, an allogeneic CD19 CAR T therapy, for treating MS. This is in Phase 1 development. This is a very speculative venture that reflects a broader industry trend of evaluating cell therapies in autoimmune conditions.

Financial Health

Taking a closer look at Q4 earnings, total revenues were $108.2 million. Cost of revenue was $15.8 million. SG&A and R&D expenses totaled $38.963 million and $23.874 million, respectively. Net income for Q4 was $23.33 million, compared to a net loss of $14.416 million in Q4 2023. Notably, the rate of expense growth (+35% year-over-year) was outpaced by total revenue growth (+41%, excluding the impact of one-time license in 2023), highlighting Briumvi’s adoption and the company’s operating leverage.

As of December 31, TGTX held $311 million in cash, cash equivalents, and investment securities. It owes $244.429 million in loan payable, but maintains total stockholders’ equity of $222.364 million.

Valuation Assumptions

I ran a discounted cash flow (DCF) analysis to get an idea about the expectations implied by TGTX’s valuation. As for inputs, I felt 20% revenue growth and 25% free cash flow margins were reasonable long-term goals. Note that TGTX had a negative FCF margin in 2024. 25%, although reasonable, is an optimistic assumption based on historical financial numbers for mature pharmaceutical companies. 20% revenue growth would exceed MS market size CAGR of 7.9%. Please note that 20% represents an “average” over the nine-year projection period. In reality, TGTX’s growth will not be linear.

So, 20% growth assumes that TGTX is taking a lot of market share from its competitors. “Cost of debt” and “tax rate” are estimates (actual figures depend on market conditions, the company's performance, etc.) based on available information (10-K) and do not have much impact on its overall discount rate as TGTX is predominately equity-based. The stock beta of 1.5 (over the past 24 months) is derived from Seeking Alpha data. Some sources have TGTX beta as being higher (depending on the timeline). The terminal growth % of 2.5 is a standard model assumption. For a company with one product so far, it is optimistic to assume it will have any terminal growth beyond the projection period.

Even based on optimistic assumptions, TGTX is overvalued by nearly 30%.

Next, I conduct a sensitivity analysis to assess how key changes to inputs (revenue growth and FCF margins) impact valuation. This also acts as a “reverse DCF model.”

TGTX appears to be priced for 26.4% revenue growth (assuming 25% FCF margins).

Finally, my Monte Carlo simulation accounts for variation among key inputs like growth, margins, beta, and discount rate to provide a more dynamic and realistic view of share price outcomes.

Again, these are based on my assumptions and garbage in = garbage out when it comes to financial models.

The key takeaway is this: TGTX is already priced for a lot of success (>25% year-over-year revenue growth and FCF margins).

Conclusion

To wrap things up, TGTX’s Briumvi is outperforming internal and analyst expectations in the competitive MS market. Granted, much of this is accounted for in its valuation, which has jumped over 170% within the past year.

Importantly, biologics have the advantage of 12-year market exclusivity and their prices do not suffer much (relative to small molecules) when biosimilars are introduced. This gives TGTX a lot of time to benefit from Briumvi revenues. Meanwhile, evaluating ublituximab as a subcutaneous injection follows market trends and may confer more years of market exclusivity. Reaching profitability has also allowed TGTX to comfortably expand beyond just ublituximab in MS. Efforts in other autoimmune conditions (e.g., MG) and treatment modalities (e.g., cell therapy) allow for optionality and aim to expand its position in the large and growing autoimmune market. That being said, per reverse DCF, TGTX appears reasonably priced at the moment, meriting a rating of Hold and market perform.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
